summary: 44 tests across 4 files (utils, iron-law-static, scheduling, aspect-resolver) all pass — but only after a fresh `npm install` because the vitest binary was missing from node_modules (CI runs `npm ci` so CI is unaffected; a local dev running `npm test` cold gets exit 127, not a real result). The iron-law static-grep guards are real and currently catch the documented forbidden patterns, but they have verified blind spots: a NEW unguarded Supabase call keyed on cardId would not be detected, and the load() fallback guard only matches the exact `result.data.length > 0` shape so refactors to `result.data?.length` or `length !== 0` slip past. Critically, none of the six write paths in pipeline-context.tsx, the workspace provisioner, the auth helpers, or any of 33 API routes have a single runtime test — the source-to-test ratio is 123:4.

findings.title: Iron-law §5 guard test has a blind spot: a NEW unguarded Supabase call on cardId is not caught
findings.description: The §5 test asserts `PIPELINE_SRC.match(/isValidUuid\s*\(\s*cardId\s*\)/g).length >= 5`. This only counts guard occurrences. Verified adversarially: inserting a new `supabase.from('posts').update({...}).eq('id', cardId)` without any isValidUuid guard leaves the count at 5, so the test STILL PASSES. AGENTS.md §5 explicitly forbids 'new Supabase calls that bypass it' — exactly the regression this test fails to detect. A temp-id (timestamp string) reaching a UUID column produces a PostgREST 400 that triggers the optimistic rollback, snapping the card back: the documented data-loss incident.
findings.reproducer: Add any `supabase.from("posts").<op>().eq("id", cardId)` call in pipeline-context.tsx without wrapping it in `if (useSupabase && isValidUuid(cardId))`. The static test passes; the card snaps back on every mutation for temp-id cards.
findings.recommendedFix: In src/lib/__tests__/iron-law-static.test.ts, add a test that counts ALL `.eq("id", cardId)` occurrences and asserts that count equals the number of `isValidUuid(cardId)` guards (every id-keyed Supabase op must have a matching guard). Alternatively, assert that every `supabase.from("posts").(update|delete)` whose chain contains `cardId` is textually preceded within ~600 chars by `isValidUuid(cardId)`.

findings.title: Iron-law §1b load() guard only catches the literal `result.data.length > 0` shape — trivial refactors bypass it
findings.description: The §1b test forbids `/result\.data\s*&&\s*result\.data\.length\s*>\s*0[\s\S]{0,400}else[\s\S]{0,200}setCards\(\s*loadState/`. Verified adversarially: a regression written as `if (!result.error && result.data?.length) { ... } else { setCards(loadState(...)) }` is NOT matched (optional-chaining form). Likewise `result.data.length !== 0` is NOT matched. Both are the exact AGENTS.md §1b forbidden behavior — falling back to PLACEHOLDER_CARDS on an empty DB result, which makes a user's whole board appear to vanish. The test also has NO positive assertion that load() uses `result.data` directly, so it cannot confirm the correct pattern is present, only that one specific bad spelling is absent.
findings.reproducer: Rewrite the load() result branch as `if (!result.error && result.data?.length) { setCards(result.data.map(dbToCard)); } else { setCards(loadState(STORAGE_KEY, PLACEHOLDER_CARDS)); }`. npm test stays green; an authenticated user with zero posts sees PLACEHOLDER_CARDS instead of an empty board, masking real (empty) data.
findings.recommendedFix: In iron-law-static.test.ts, replace the single forbidden regex with: (1) a positive assertion that the load() body contains `result.data) {` followed by `setCards(result.data.map`, and (2) a broadened forbidden regex covering `result.data?.length`, `result.data.length > 0`, `result.data.length !== 0`, and `result.data.length >= 1` all flowing into an `else` that calls `setCards(loadState`. Better: extract the load-result-handling into a pure exported function `resolveLoadedCards(result)` and unit-test it directly with {error, data:[]}, {error:err, data:null}, {error:null, data:[rows]}.

findings.title: Duplicate WEAK isValidUuid in media-assets.ts violates AGENTS.md §5 and is untested
findings.description: media-assets.ts defines its own local `isValidUuid` with regex `/^[0-9a-f]{8}-[0-9a-f]{4}-[0-9a-f]{4}-[0-9a-f]{4}-[0-9a-f]{12}$/i` — NO version nibble [1-5] and NO variant nibble [89ab] enforcement. AGENTS.md §5 mandates the strict shared guard from utils.ts (which IS unit-tested in utils.test.ts and explicitly rejects the zero-UUID and unversioned shapes). This weak copy accepts `00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000` and `12345678-1234-0234-c234-123456789abc` — both rejected by the canonical guard. It gates the `used_in` post-id append on media_assets (an iron-law §2 table). The utils.test.ts suite gives false confidence that 'isValidUuid is covered' when this divergent copy is exercised by the media path and has zero tests.
findings.reproducer: Call ensureMediaAsset with usedIn = '00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000'. The local weak isValidUuid returns true, so the zero-UUID gets pushed into the media_assets.used_in array — the strict canonical guard would have rejected it.
findings.recommendedFix: media-assets.ts should `import { isValidUuid } from "./utils"` and delete lines 3-7. Then add a static-grep test (or extend iron-law-static.test.ts) that fails if any file under src/lib defines a local `function isValidUuid` or a `UUID_REGEX` const, forcing a single source of truth. Add a unit test asserting the media path rejects the zero-UUID.

findings.title: No test covers any of the six pipeline-context.tsx write paths or their optimistic-rollback branches
findings.description: createCard, moveCard, updateCard, deleteCard, submitReapproval, and submitKickback contain the entire data-integrity surface of the app — optimistic setCards, Supabase write, error-branch rollback, toast, markMutation dedup, publish-job creation gate, and the createCard tempId-to-real-UUID swap. NONE of it has a runtime test. The iron-law-static test only greps for forbidden text; it never executes a single line of these functions. Every rollback branch (e.g. createCard removing the tempId card on insert error, deleteCard re-inserting `previousCard` on a protect-trigger rejection, moveCard's nested rollback-of-rollback at lines 531-545) is unverified. A future edit that drops a rollback or mis-orders setCards would not fail any test. AGENTS.md §1c's exact-fallback requirement for workspace_id is only checked as a string match, not by executing createCard with workspaceIdRef.current === null.
findings.reproducer: Break createCard's error branch (remove `setCards((prev) => prev.filter((c) => c.id !== tempId))`) so a failed insert leaves a phantom tempId card that vanishes on next refresh. npm test stays green.
findings.recommendedFix: Add src/lib/__tests__/pipeline-context.test.tsx using @testing-library/react (already a devDependency). Render PipelineProvider with mocked supabaseClient/auth-context/toast-context. Test: (a) createCard with workspaceIdRef null still inserts workspace_id === baseline UUID; (b) createCard insert error removes the tempId card and fires an error toast; (c) deleteCard with a protect-trigger error message re-inserts previousCard; (d) moveCard to 'posted' short-circuits with a warning toast and no DB write; (e) updateCard DB error restores previousCard. Mock supabase's chained `.from().update().eq()` to resolve {error}.

findings.title: vitest binary missing from node_modules — `npm test` returns exit 127, not a test result, on a cold checkout
findings.description: On a fresh clone (and on this audited working copy before remediation), `node_modules` existed but `node_modules/.bin/vitest` did not — `npm test` produced `sh: vitest: command not found` with exit code 127. CI runs `npm ci` first so CI itself is safe, but: (1) any developer who runs `npm test` locally without a clean install gets a false 'no output' and may assume tests pass; (2) the preflight script chains `npm ci && ... && npm test` so it self-heals, but the bare `npm test` script does not. This is a tooling-reliability gap that lets a developer ship believing tests ran.
findings.reproducer: On a checkout where devDependencies are partially installed, run `npm test`. Output: `sh: vitest: command not found`, exit 127. A `| tail` pipe (common in scripts) masks the 127 as a 0.
findings.recommendedFix: This is an environment issue, not a code defect — no source change strictly required. To harden: confirm CI always runs `npm ci` (it does). Optionally add a `pretest` script that runs `node -e "require('vitest')"` or checks `node_modules/.bin/vitest` exists and prints a clear 'run npm install' message. Document in AGENTS.md that `npm test` assumes a complete install.

findings.title: scheduling.test.ts tests a COPY of toScheduledAt, not the real production function
findings.description: scheduling.test.ts re-implements toScheduledAt inline ('Mirror of pipeline-context.tsx#toScheduledAt — keep in sync') because the real one is a private, non-exported helper in pipeline-context.tsx. The 10 tests exercise the COPY. If someone edits the real toScheduledAt in pipeline-context.tsx (e.g. reverts the DST offset math, the exact regression test at line 90-95 claims to guard), the real function breaks and every card scheduled into approved_scheduled gets the wrong scheduled_at — but all 10 tests still pass because they never touch the real code. The test file's own TODO admits this. scheduled_at drives the n8n auto-publisher; a wrong value means posts publish at the wrong time.
findings.reproducer: In pipeline-context.tsx, delete the offset math in toScheduledAt and `return naive.toISOString()` directly. cardToDb now writes 14:30Z instead of 19:30Z for a 14:30 CDT input. scheduling.test.ts stays 10/10 green because it tests its own private copy.
findings.recommendedFix: Export toScheduledAt from pipeline-context.tsx (or move it to src/lib/utils.ts as the test's TODO already says) and change scheduling.test.ts line 17 region to `import { toScheduledAt } from "../pipeline-context"`. Delete the inline mirror. The existing 10 assertions then exercise real production code.

findings.title: audit.ts §3 guard does not cover post_audit_logs writes in OTHER files; toAuditEntry fallback chain untested
findings.description: Two gaps. (1) The iron-law §3 static test only reads src/lib/audit.ts. AGENTS.md §3 forbids client-side writes to post_audit_logs ANYWHERE. A future client component that does `supabase.from('post_audit_logs').insert(...)` would be a silent-drop data-loss bug and no test would catch it (the test's scope is one file). Note: current post_audit_logs references in deep-check/route.ts and settings-page.tsx are reads/health-probes, which §3 permits — but nothing enforces that they stay reads. (2) toAuditEntry's 6-level actor-name fallback chain (actor_name -> user_name -> movedBy -> approvedBy -> changedBy -> actor_role -> 'Unknown') and the dual-format revision-history parsing have zero tests. A reordering would silently show 'Unknown' or the wrong author on audit rows.
findings.reproducer: Add `supabase.from('post_audit_logs').insert({...})` inside any component .tsx file. The §3 test (which only reads audit.ts) passes; the insert is silently blocked by RLS and the audit entry is lost.
findings.recommendedFix: Broaden the §3 test to grep ALL of src for `from(['"]post_audit_logs['"]).(insert|update|delete|upsert)` (not just audit.ts), allowing `.select` reads. Separately, export toAuditEntry (or test via fetchAuditLogs with a mocked supabase) and add unit tests for each fallback rung and for both revision-note formats.

findings.title: Workspace provisioner (/api/workspace/provision) has no test — the iron-law §4 RLS gate is unverified
findings.description: The provisioner is called on every app load and is the self-healing fix for the RLS chicken-and-egg that AGENTS.md §4 and §1b depend on. It has security-critical branches: 401 on missing token, 401 on bad token, SEC-006 403-reject for users with no team_members row (explicitly added to stop self-service signup), 403 for empty role, the SEC-018 upsert-with-ignoreDuplicates dual-tab race fix, and the SEC-014 email hashing. None is tested. A regression that reverts SEC-006 (auto-creating an editor row for any authenticated user) re-opens the self-service-signup hole with zero test failure. A regression in the upsert onConflict re-introduces the dual-tab unique-violation crash.
findings.reproducer: Change `if (!tm) return 403` back to auto-inserting a pending editor. No test fails; the provisioner silently becomes an open signup endpoint again.
findings.recommendedFix: Add src/app/api/workspace/__tests__/provision.test.ts. Mock @supabase/supabase-js createClient. Call GET with: (a) no Authorization header -> expect 401; (b) token whose getUser errors -> 401; (c) valid user with no team_members row -> 403 'Not on team'; (d) team_members row with empty role -> 403; (e) valid active team member -> 200 with workspaceId and provisioned:true, and assert upsert was called with onConflict 'user_id,workspace_id' and ignoreDuplicates true.

findings.title: Auth helpers requireRole / requireBearerTeamRole are wired into 12 routes but have no tests
findings.description: require.ts header comment says 'NOT yet wired to any route' — that comment is now STALE. Grep confirms requireBearerTeamRole / requireRole / requireBearerUser are imported by 12 API routes including team/invite, team/remove-member, team/approve-request, ai/studio/access, and all four notifications/* routes. These helpers are the access-control boundary for those endpoints, and their fail-safe branches (error||!data -> 403, role-not-in-allowed -> 403, catch -> 403, missing env -> 401) are entirely untested. A regression that flips a fail-safe to fail-open (e.g. `if (error || !data) return ctx-with-role` ) would silently grant unauthorized access with no test failure.
findings.reproducer: Change requireRole's `if (error || !data) return forbidden(...)` to fall through with a default role. The 12 protected routes now accept callers with no workspace membership. No test catches it.
findings.recommendedFix: Add src/lib/auth/__tests__/require.test.ts. Mock createServerSupabaseClient and createClient. Test requireUser (no session -> 401), requireRole (no workspace_members row -> 403, wrong role -> 403, correct role -> AuthContext), requireBearerUser (no header -> 401, bad token -> 401), requireBearerTeamRole (non-admin role -> 403, admin -> {user,email,role}). Also delete the stale 'NOT yet wired' comment.

findings.title: Zero tests for any of the 33 API routes including notification, drive, publish-jobs and AI-studio endpoints
findings.description: find counts 33 route.ts files; zero have a colocated test. This includes notifications/* (which the iron-law data-loss incidents reference for phantom emails — DATA-002), publish-jobs (creates the publish job whose absence is the 'orphan-approved-no-job hole' called out in moveCard), drive/* upload routes (c09439b's commit message says these had unauth holes that were fixed — a fix with no test invites regression), and ai/studio/* (per-row $3 cost cap, STUDIO_ENABLED kill switch). These all carry auth checks, rate limits, and cost guards that can silently regress.
findings.reproducer: Any auth/cost/rate-limit guard in any of the 33 routes can be weakened or removed and the full `npm test` run stays green — there is no coverage at all on the route layer.
findings.recommendedFix: Prioritize tests for the highest-blast-radius routes first: api/publish-jobs (assert it rejects a post with no scheduled_at and creates a job otherwise), api/notifications/* (assert they only send to safeRecipients-validated addresses and require auth), api/drive/upload + proxy-upload + finalize (assert unauthenticated requests get 401). Use Next's route handler test pattern with a mocked NextRequest.

findings.title: dbToCard — used by load() and all three realtime handlers — has no test for its revision-history regex parsing
findings.description: dbToCard maps every DB row to a ContentCard and runs on the load() path AND on every realtime INSERT/UPDATE. It reconstructs `revised` and `revisionHistory` by running two matchAll regexes over the notes field (old format `Revision Note (...)` and new format `... (...): Fix submitted — ...`). A bug here mis-renders revision history on every card after a refresh. It is a non-exported pure function with zero tests. The lookahead `(?=\n\n|$)` and the interplay of the two formats are exactly the kind of logic that breaks silently.
findings.reproducer: Notes containing both an old-format and a new-format revision entry, or a malformed entry, produce a wrong revisionHistory array. No test exists to catch a regex change.
findings.recommendedFix: Export dbToCard (or a `parseRevisionHistory(notes)` helper) and add unit tests: notes with one old-format entry, one new-format entry, both, none, and a malformed entry. Assert the `revised` boolean and the revisionHistory array shape.

findings.title: email-utils security functions (esc, safeSubject, isValidEmail, safeRecipients) untested despite being injection guards
findings.description: esc() prevents HTML/XSS injection in email bodies, safeSubject() strips CR/LF to block header injection, isValidEmail()/safeRecipients() reject addresses smuggling CR/LF/commas before they reach nodemailer to/cc/bcc. These are security boundaries used by every email template and notification route. None has a test. A regression that drops the `'` replacement in esc, or loosens the EMAIL_RE to allow a newline, re-opens an injection vector with no test failure. These are pure functions — the cheapest possible thing to test well.
findings.reproducer: Remove the `.replace(/[\r\n]+/g, ' ')` from safeSubject; a subject containing `\r\nBcc: attacker@evil.com` now injects a header. No test catches it.
findings.recommendedFix: Add src/lib/__tests__/email-utils.test.ts. Test esc() escapes & < > " '; safeSubject() strips CR/LF and truncates at 200; isValidEmail() rejects '', strings over 320 chars, addresses with commas/CRLF/angle-brackets, and accepts a normal address; safeRecipients() dedupes case-insensitively and drops invalid entries.

findings.title: rate-limit.ts consume() fail-open behavior and getClientIp precedence untested
findings.description: consume() deliberately fails OPEN (returns allowed:true) on any infra error so a rate-limit outage does not take down the protected endpoint — a deliberate, security-relevant trade-off. getClientIp() has a defined precedence (cf-connecting-ip -> x-forwarded-for first entry -> x-real-ip -> 'unknown'). Neither is tested. A regression that makes consume() fail CLOSED would silently 429 real users during a transient DB blip; a regression in getClientIp precedence would rate-limit the wrong key (e.g. all users behind one proxy share a bucket). Both are pure-ish and easily tested.
findings.reproducer: Change consume()'s catch block to `return { allowed: false, ... }`. Every protected endpoint now hard-fails during any Supabase hiccup. No test catches the inverted behavior.
findings.recommendedFix: Add src/lib/__tests__/rate-limit.test.ts. For consume(): mock the admin client rpc to throw and to return {error} — assert allowed:true (fail-open) in both cases; mock a normal row and assert allowed reflects it. For getClientIp(): build Request objects with each header combination and assert the precedence and the 'unknown' fallback.

findings.title: Iron-law §1b provision-before-SELECT test verifies TEXT order only, and the provision fetch is in a conditional else-branch
findings.description: The §1b test extracts the load() body and asserts `indexOf('/api/workspace/provision') < indexOf('supabase.from("posts").select')`. Two weaknesses: (1) it checks the textual position of the string, not the runtime call order — and in the current source the provision fetch lives inside an `else` fallback branch (lines 333-347) that only runs when `provisionResult?.workspaceId` is absent. When provisionResult IS pre-fetched, no provision fetch happens in load() at all; the test still passes because the string '/api/workspace/provision' appears in the dead-ish else branch. (2) The match anchor `hydrated.current = true` is brittle: moving that assignment breaks the whole test with a confusing failure unrelated to the guard. The test gives weaker assurance than it appears to.
findings.reproducer: If a refactor removes the provision-fetch else-branch entirely (relying only on provisionResult from AuthProvider), the textual string disappears and the test fails — even though provisioning still happens upstream. Conversely, the string could be left in a comment and the test would pass with no real provisioning.
findings.recommendedFix: Strengthen the §1b test to also assert the provision string is NOT inside a comment, and add a note that provisioning may legitimately happen upstream in AuthProvider. Better long-term: an integration test rendering PipelineProvider that asserts the /api/workspace/provision fetch (or provisionResult consumption) resolves before the posts SELECT fires, using a mock that records call order.

findings.title: No coverage threshold enforced — @vitest/coverage-v8 is installed but CI never gates on it
findings.description: package.json has a test:coverage script and @vitest/coverage-v8 is a devDependency, but ci.yml runs plain `npm test` with no coverage gate and vitest.config.ts sets no `coverage.thresholds`. With 4 test files against 123 source files, coverage is effectively a rounding error, and nothing stops it from drifting to zero. There is no signal in CI when a large new untested module lands.
findings.reproducer: Add a new 500-line untested module. CI stays green and gives no coverage warning.
findings.recommendedFix: Either add `coverage: { thresholds: { lines: N, functions: N } }` to vitest.config.ts and run `npm run test:coverage` in CI, or at minimum add a CI step that prints the coverage summary so reviewers see the trend. Start the threshold low and ratchet up.

findings.title: normalizePublishJob and cardToDb (pure mappers) have no tests
findings.description: normalizePublishJob handles the array-vs-single and nested platform_publish_attempts normalization that backs the publish-status UI (and is exactly the shape affected by AGENTS.md §7's POSTS_SELECT_FULL/BASIC fallback). cardToDb maps a partial ContentCard to a DB update payload and contains the scheduled_at derivation branch at line 218. Both are pure and untested. A bug in cardToDb's `card.scheduledDate !== undefined || card.scheduledTime !== undefined` condition would write a stale or null scheduled_at on an unrelated field edit.
findings.reproducer: normalizePublishJob given a single (non-array) publish_jobs object, or a single attempt object, could mis-normalize and break the publish-status badge. No test exists.
findings.recommendedFix: Export both helpers and add unit tests: normalizePublishJob with array input, single-object input, null, and single vs array attempts; cardToDb asserting scheduled_at is set only when scheduledDate or scheduledTime is present and is null when they are cleared.
